### Issue

I have some code using Cantera that runs (on Windows 10) as expected when using openblas, but starts segfaulting as soon as I [switch](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base/#switching-blas-implementation) to blis. I am avoiding use of MKL by installing `nomkl`.

At this point I am not 100% sure this is a blis packaging issue, but the crash happens within blis code.
I noticed that there are some issues in the blis error tracker regarding access violations, although for a Haswell CPU architecture, and I'm on Tigerlake. Nevertheless, the latest version 1.0 that has fixed those is not yet packaged for Conda-forge (#33), so I cannot confirm.

Reproduction:

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2024-06-13T11:09:35Z","updated_at":"2024-12-06T10:37:55Z","closed_at":"2024-11-16T22:44:47Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When using `callstatement` to overrride a subroutine with different code as per [the docs ](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/f2py/signature-file.html#f2py-statements), this works when said subroutine is directly invoked by Python. However, when invoking another subroutine that calls the first subroutine, this override is not in effect.

MWE:
test.f90
```fortran
module utils
    implicit none
  contains
    subroutine my_abort(message)
      implicit none
      character(len=*), intent(in) :: message
      !f2py callstatement PyErr_SetString(PyExc_ValueError, message);f2py_success = 0;
      !f2py callprotoargument char*
      write(0,*) "THIS SHOULD NOT APPEAR"
      stop 1
    end subroutine my_abort

    subroutine do_something()
        call my_abort("aborting inside")
    end subroutine do_something
end module utils
```

To avoid #2547, you have to do the compilation in 2 steps:
```
python3 -m numpy.f2py -h test.pyf --no-lower -m test test.f90
python3 -m numpy.f2py -c --f90exec=gfortran --fcompiler=gnu95 test.pyf test.f90
```

Invoking `my_abort` directly works as expected, raising the ValueError I have defined:
```
$ python3 -c "import test; test.utils.my_abort('aborting directly')"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
ValueError: aborting directly
```

However, when calling `do_something`, which in turn calls `my_abort`, the override seems to do nothing, and we run into the `stop`:
```
$ python3 -c "import test; test.utils.do_something()"
 THIS SHOULD NOT APPEAR
STOP 1
```
Expected result: Python raises `ValueError: aborting inside`


### Reproduce the code example:

```python
python3 -c "import test; test.utils.do_something()"
 THIS SHOULD NOT APPEAR
STOP 1

# Expected result: Python raises `ValueError: aborting inside`
```

### Context for the issue:

What I'm intending here is to avoid executing the Fortran `stop` statement when running wrapped code in Python, because that also kills the Python *interpreter* and thus, in my case, the `pytest` run of a whole test suite that this code is part of.

This `my_abort` cleanup subroutine is potentially called from many places throughout the actual codebase.

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-06-13T11:09:35Z","updated_at":"2024-11-04T09:20:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When using `callstatement` to overrride a subroutine with different code as per [the docs ](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/f2py/signature-file.html#f2py-statements), this works when said subroutine is directly invoked by Python. However, when invoking another subroutine that calls the first subroutine, this override is not in effect.

MWE:
test.f90
```fortran
module utils
    implicit none
  contains
    subroutine my_abort(message)
      implicit none
      character(len=*), intent(in) :: message
      !f2py callstatement PyErr_SetString(PyExc_ValueError, message);f2py_success = 0;
      !f2py callprotoargument char*
      write(0,*) "THIS SHOULD NOT APPEAR"
      stop 1
    end subroutine my_abort

    subroutine do_something()
        call my_abort("aborting inside")
    end subroutine do_something
end module utils
```

To avoid #2547, you have to do the compilation in 2 steps:
```
python3 -m numpy.f2py -h test.pyf --no-lower -m test test.f90
python3 -m numpy.f2py -c --f90exec=gfortran --fcompiler=gnu95 test.pyf test.f90
```

Invoking `my_abort` directly works as expected, raising the ValueError I have defined:
```
$ python3 -c "import test; test.utils.my_abort('aborting directly')"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
ValueError: aborting directly
```

However, when calling `do_something`, which in turn calls `my_abort`, the override seems to do nothing, and we run into the `stop`:
```
$ python3 -c "import test; test.utils.do_something()"
 THIS SHOULD NOT APPEAR
STOP 1
```
Expected result: Python raises `ValueError: aborting inside`


### Reproduce the code example:

```python
python3 -c "import test; test.utils.do_something()"
 THIS SHOULD NOT APPEAR
STOP 1

# Expected result: Python raises `ValueError: aborting inside`
```

_No response_

I don't really think this is something which F2PY can handle. Basically, when you call `my_abort` in `do_something()` it calls the Fortran subroutine, but the `callstatement` is only valid for calls from Python into `my_abort` (basically because the `my_abort` Python wrapper includes the `callstatement`).
> 
> This behavior is integral to the way F2PY works efficiently, since always calling Python wrapped variants of Fortran routines would be pretty inefficient.
> 
> The way to call the Python wrapped variant would be using callbacks. I'm open to more design ideas about this but as it stands this isn't actionable.

Sad to hear that. Can't offer design ideas because the whole space is way too confusing/intricate for me -- if you say it can't be handled, then that's how it is.

I'm not sure what you mean exactly with this idea about callbacks? Could you maybe find the time to demonstrate this by adapting the MWE in here to that approach? 
I.e. so that `python3 -c "import test; test.utils.do_something()"` raises the `ValueError` instead of STOPping? Then I could translate that back into my real solution. 
Thanks!

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2021-10-29T10:04:28Z","updated_at":"2024-08-20T07:47:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

Although changes have been made to make some commands more portable (`f2py`-->`python -m numpy.f2py` , there are still no clear indications as to the method of using F2PY on windows.

As of this issue, there are only [two compilers](https://fortran-lang.org/compilers) which have first class support for Windows:
- Silverfrost (F95 only)
- Intel oneAPI

The most common approach is to mix an existing version of MSVC with minGW from either binary installs or a management system like Choco or MSYS2.

These various installation methods combined with differing pythons (from `conda`, from the Windows store, from Intel etc.) which are the source of many fun bugs like the seven or [more ones here](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues?q=is%3Aopen+is%3Aissue+label%3A%22component%3A+numpy.f2py%22+windows).

### Idea or request for content:

A single comprehensive source of truth regarding Windows usage for F2PY. Essentially I'm thinking of (with VS-Code as the IDE):
- WSL
- Intel everything
- MSYS + MSVC (recommended by VS-Code)
- Choco + MSVC

Combined with the following Python sources:
- Conda
- Intel Python
- Windows store Python

Let me know if something is missing.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/20227/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/20227/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2298194146","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/20227#issuecomment-2298194146","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/20227","id":2298194146,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86I-6ji","user":{"login":"bilderbuchi","id":327442,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNzQ0Mg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/327442?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi","html_url":"https://github.com/bilderbuchi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-20T07:47:36Z","updated_at":"2024-08-20T07:47:36Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> Until there's movement on [conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io#1044](https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044), I think we'll be stuck with gcc 5.3.0, though (which is ~ the only reason why I'm using MSYS...)

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044/events","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044","id":607462973,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU2MDc0NjI5NzM=","number":1044,"title":"Self-sufficient MinGW-based toolchain / packages","user":{"login":"xhochy","id":70274,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcwMjc0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/70274?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy","html_url":"https://github.com/xhochy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":12,"created_at":"2020-04-27T11:18:47Z","updated_at":"2024-08-20T07:50:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We currently rely on the `msys2` channel for providing a MinGW-based toolchain. The main usage of this is currently to build R and its packages. The `msys2` channel hasn't been updated for more than 3 years and we are also missing some native dependencies like `m2w64-pcre2` in it for the latest R release. To make conda-forge self-sufficient on Windows, we should get rid of the `msys2` channel dependency and build up our own set of packages for this.

There are two approaches I could think of, each with its different problems:

1. Build up our own MinGW toolchain from scratch and compile all packages from source. We could also make the compiler as a matrix so that we build some packages in the same recipe for MSVC and MinGW. The disadvantage here is that we will replicate all the work from MSYS2. On the other side this better fits into our infrastructure and would also allow us to apply our own modifications to the source build.
2. Repackage binaries from [MSYS2](https://www.msys2.org/) as the `msys2` channel has done in the past. This is the easier approach but we need to think of which packages should be included. All? Only a fixed list?

Approach 2 can probably be scripted mostly using the metadata from MSYS2 and would be the path to go for now to get things up and running.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/comments/2298200082","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044#issuecomment-2298200082","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044","id":2298200082,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAlKGKs6I-8AS","user":{"login":"bilderbuchi","id":327442,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNzQ0Mg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/327442?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi","html_url":"https://github.com/bilderbuchi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-20T07:50:32Z","updated_at":"2024-08-20T07:50:32Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"So, {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26681","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26681/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26681/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26681/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26681","id":2350862410,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86MH1BK","number":26681,"title":"f2py callstatement is ineffective in subroutines called from other subroutines","user":{"login":"bilderbuchi","id":327442,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNzQ0Mg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/327442?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi","html_url":"https://github.com/bilderbuchi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-06-13T11:09:35Z","updated_at":"2024-06-13T11:09:35Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When using `callstatement` to overrride a subroutine with different code as per [the docs ](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/f2py/signature-file.html#f2py-statements), this works when said subroutine is directly invoked by Python. However, when invoking another subroutine that calls the first subroutine, this override is not in effect.

MWE:
test.f90
```fortran
module utils
    implicit none
  contains
    subroutine my_abort(message)
      implicit none
      character(len=*), intent(in) :: message
      !f2py callstatement PyErr_SetString(PyExc_ValueError, message);f2py_success = 0;
      !f2py callprotoargument char*
      write(0,*) "THIS SHOULD NOT APPEAR"
      stop 1
    end subroutine my_abort
end module utils

module nest
    use utils, only: my_abort
    implicit none
  contains
    subroutine do_something()
      call my_abort("aborting inside")
    end subroutine do_something
end module nest
```

To avoid #2547, you have to do the compilation in 2 steps:
```
python3 -m numpy.f2py -h test.pyf --no-lower -m test test.f90
python3 -m numpy.f2py -c --f90exec=gfortran --fcompiler=gnu95 test.pyf test.f90
```

Invoking `my_abort` directly works as expected, raising the ValueError I have defined:
```
$ python3 -c "import test; test.utils.my_abort('aborting directly')"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
ValueError: aborting directly
```

However, when calling `do_something`, which in turn calls `my_abort`, the override seems to do nothing, and we run into the `stop`:
```

When using `callstatement` to overrride a subroutine with different code as per [the docs ](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/f2py/signature-file.html#f2py-statements), this works when said subroutine is directly invoked by Python. However, when invoking another subroutine that calls the first subroutine, this override is not in effect.

MWE:
test.f90
```fortran
module utils
    implicit none
  contains
    subroutine my_abort(message)
      implicit none
      character(len=*), intent(in) :: message
      !f2py callstatement PyErr_SetString(PyExc_ValueError, message);f2py_success = 0;
      !f2py callprotoargument char*
      write(0,*) "THIS SHOULD NOT APPEAR"
      stop 1
    end subroutine my_abort

    subroutine do_something()
        call my_abort("aborting inside")
    end subroutine do_something
end module utils
```

To avoid #2547, you have to do the compilation in 2 steps:
```
python3 -m numpy.f2py -h test.pyf --no-lower -m test test.f90
python3 -m numpy.f2py -c --f90exec=gfortran --fcompiler=gnu95 test.pyf test.f90
```

Invoking `my_abort` directly works as expected, raising the ValueError I have defined:
```
$ python3 -c "import test; test.utils.my_abort('aborting directly')"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
ValueError: aborting directly
```

However, when calling `do_something`, which in turn calls `my_abort`, the override seems to do nothing, and we run into the `stop`:
```
$ python3 -c "import test; test.utils.do_something()"
 THIS SHOULD NOT APPEAR
STOP 1
```
Expected result: Python raises `ValueError: aborting inside`


### Reproduce the code example:

```python
python3 -c "import test; test.utils.do_something()"
 THIS SHOULD NOT APPEAR
STOP 1

# Expected result: Python raises `ValueError: aborting inside`
```

your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
`grayskull` will identify licenses by fuzzy match in some cases and then suggest the best result. However, sometimes the fuzzy match scores a bit lower than average because some people _modify_ popular licenses like BSD or MIT and add their own clauses. This kind of change will turn those into a different license altogether, which should be named as `LicenseRef-packagename`.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
I suggest:

* Adding a comment next to the suggested identifier, saying that its match % was lower than usual `license: MIT  # warning! license might have been modified; search match is XX%`.
* If no clear license was found, generate a `LicenseRef-packagename` identifier with a comment next to it like `# license could not be identified, please review`.


**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Erroring out seems excessive.

**Additional context**

A recent [example](https://github.com/gdellaire/BBii-Decon/blob/18563b2bb823977ffa19628c6f7cdb6fe0b35ca3/LICENSE#L20-L22) I found on a conda-forge submission.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/grayskull/issues/303/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/grayskull/issues/303/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/grayskull/issues/comments/2126506342","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/grayskull/issues/303#issuecomment-2126506342","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/grayskull/issues/303","id":2126506342,"node_id":"IC_kwDODdg1y85-v-lm","user":{"login":"bilderbuchi","id":327442,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNzQ0Mg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/327442?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi","html_url":"https://github.com/bilderbuchi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-23T08:16:49Z","updated_at":"2024-05-23T08:16:49Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"It seems to me that grayskull should not put too weak assumptions into the generated recipe.

In my case, I'm running grayskull over an sdist with a proprietary license, but grayskull does

> Matching license file with database from Grayskull...
Match percentage of the license is 9%. Low match percentage could mean that the license was modified.
License type: BSD-2-Clause
License file: ['LICENSE.txt']

and puts 
> about:
  license: BSD-2-Clause
  license_file: LICENSE.txt

into the recipe. 

While of course the LICENSE.txt should be the final (correct) truth, putting this down as a `BSD-2-Clause` in the package metadata is **very** far away from the truth. 
I think the second bullet above about what to do when no clear license could be identified is very relevant, I'd expect a `license: LicenseRef-LICENSE.txt` or similar in my case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/grayskull/issues/comments/2126506342/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Perhaps I'm missing something obvious but currently grayskull allows generating recipes for a pypi package (though it accepts a github URL) or a CRAN package.

It would be nice to be able to point it to a project available locally eg; using a PEP621 pyproject.toml setuptools project. This is especially useful when dual-releasing internal packages that may not be on a public github.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
`grayskull local /path/to/pyproject.toml` generates a recipe given a pyproject.toml.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
N/A

**Additional context**
Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

> grayskull pypi ./dist/*.tar.gz

your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
`grayskull` will identify licenses by fuzzy match in some cases and then suggest the best result. However, sometimes the fuzzy match scores a bit lower than average because some people _modify_ popular licenses like BSD or MIT and add their own clauses. This kind of change will turn those into a different license altogether, which should be named as `LicenseRef-packagename`.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
I suggest:

* Adding a comment next to the suggested identifier, saying that its match % was lower than usual `license: MIT  # warning! license might have been modified; search match is XX%`.
* If no clear license was found, generate a `LicenseRef-packagename` identifier with a comment next to it like `# license could not be identified, please review`.


**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Erroring out seems excessive.

**Additional context**

A recent [example](https://github.com/gdellaire/BBii-Decon/blob/18563b2bb823977ffa19628c6f7cdb6fe0b35ca3/LICENSE#L20-L22) I found on a conda-forge submission.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/grayskull/issues/303/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/grayskull/issues/303/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/grayskull/issues/comments/2126639307","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/grayskull/issues/303#issuecomment-2126639307","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/grayskull/issues/303","id":2126639307,"node_id":"IC_kwDODdg1y85-wfDL","user":{"login":"bilderbuchi","id":327442,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNzQ0Mg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/327442?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi","html_url":"https://github.com/bilderbuchi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-23T09:23:02Z","updated_at":"2024-05-23T09:23:02Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> Additionally, I've found no way to override the license detector when invoking grayskull, which could be a stopgap measure to avoid putting wrong metadata in the recipe. Is there one already that I missed?

For anybody finding this in the future, one workaround I found when using `conda build` afterwards, one can use this option to override the wrong license entry with an appropriate file, so at least the built package is correct:
```
  --clobber-file CLOBBER_SECTIONS_FILE
                        Clobber data in meta.yaml with fields from this file. Jinja2 is not done on clobbered fields.
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/grayskull/issues/comments/2126639307/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

is just me proposing a suggestion from @KristofferC in https://discourse.julialang.org/t/debug-has-massive-performance-impact/103974/22, it's all his code / idea, not mine.

The idea behind this is that people have observed that [using `@debug` is significantly more costly on Windows than other platforms](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/debug-has-massive-performance-impact/103974), even though we have documented in that manual that it should be a really cheap operation. @KristofferC suggests this is due to the fact that  [checking environment variables in Windows is more costly](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/debug-has-massive-performance-impact/103974/18). 

The idea here is that we preallocate a `Cwstring` on Windows that just holds the text `"JULIA_DEBUG"`, so that if `access_env(f, "JULIA_DEBUG")` gets called, we don't need to create a new `Cwstring` and then throw it away each time. "organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"assignees":[{"login":"fredrikekre","id":11698744,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNjk4NzQ0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11698744?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre","html_url":"https://github.com/fredrikekre","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/fredrikekre/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false}],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2019-02-19T16:08:18Z","updated_at":"2023-09-29T06:16:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It would be great to have a standardised way for specifying metadata in a package's Project.toml. The following is heavily inspired by npm.

#### Package Discoverability

- `keywords`/`tags` -> array of strings (could be autopopulated from GitHubs tags by some third party utility)
- `description`/`tagline` -> string with a short (one line) description of the package

#### Utility

- `license` -> string with license name
- `version` (I know this is controversial but would still be nice to have...)
- `issue-url` -> URL where the user can report issues with the package 

#### Documentation

We need to support multiple cases here:

- `built-docs-url` -> URL to git repo + branch (+ directory) that contains built documentation
- `hosted-docs-url` -> URL to hosted docs
- `docs-directory` -> path to the dir containing the packages Documenter.jl source, falls back to `docs/`

Those three options should span all sane use cases -- we have a default where the package author doesn't have to worry about docs deployment *at all* (because we'll build them), the current standard where you set up Documenter.jl on travis yourself and have it push to a `gh-pages` branch somewhere, and an option that allows you complete freedom with your self-hosted docs.

------
So for DifferentialEquations.jl the following metadata could be supplied:
```toml
[metadata]
keywords = ["differential-equations", "julia", "ode", "sde", "pde", "dae"]
description = "Julia suite for high-performance solvers of differential equations."
license = "MIT"
issue-url = "https://github.com/JuliaDiffEq/DifferentialEquations.jl/issues/new"
version = "v6.3.0"

[metadata.documentation]
built-docs-url = "https://github.com/JuliaDiffEq/DiffEqDocs.jl.git#gh-pages"
```
`keywords`, `description`, `license` and one of the docs keys are the most important for discoverability and usability, imho, so we should heavily encourage people to fill those in, e.g. when registering a package. Would also be sensible to sanity check the different fields.

The `[metadata]` section could of course contain arbitrary information (maybe a DOI or whatever).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/1070/reactions","total_count":6,"+1":6,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/1070/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1740364303","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/1070#issuecomment-1740364303","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/1070","id":1740364303,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBOhtSc5nu9oP","user":{"login":"bilderbuchi","id":327442,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMyNzQ0Mg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/327442?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi","html_url":"https://github.com/bilderbuchi","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bilderbuchi/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-09-29T06:16:02Z","updated_at":"2023-09-29T06:16:02Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> this allows also for expressing things like "GPL 2 or later" or "dual licensed as GPL or MIT" etc.

For those not already aware, this is achieved with ["SPDX license expressions"](https://spdx.github.io/spdx-spec/v2.2.2/SPDX-license-expressions/). For the above examples, the license strings would be e.g. `GPL-2.0-or-later` (no operators needed) and `GPL-2.0-only OR MIT`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1740364303/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

- `docs-directory` -> path to the dir containing the packages Documenter.jl source, falls back to `docs/`

Those three options should span all sane use cases -- we have a default where the package author doesn't have to worry about docs deployment *at all* (because we'll build them), the current standard where you set up Documenter.jl on travis yourself and have it push to a `gh-pages` branch somewhere, and an option that allows you complete freedom with your self-hosted docs.

------
So for DifferentialEquations.jl the following metadata could be supplied:
```toml
[metadata]
keywords = ["differential-equations", "julia", "ode", "sde", "pde", "dae"]
description = "Julia suite for high-performance solvers of differential equations."
license = "MIT"
issue-url = "https://github.com/JuliaDiffEq/DifferentialEquations.jl/issues/new"
version = "v6.3.0"

[metadata.documentation]
built-docs-url = "https://github.com/JuliaDiffEq/DiffEqDocs.jl.git#gh-pages"
```
`keywords`, `description`, `license` and one of the docs keys are the most important for discoverability and usability, imho, so we should heavily encourage people to fill those in, e.g. when registering a package. Would also be sensible to sanity check the different fields.

This would enable automated discovery of the licenses to observe when installing/distributing said packages, e.g. from a `license_wrapped` key or similar (in addition to `license` for the code of the package _itself_), using the same SPDX license expression syntax as above.

For example, a wrapper package might be licensed `MIT`, but the underlying library might be licensed `LGPL` ([QML.jl](https://github.com/JuliaGraphics/QML.jl/issues/150)) or `GPL` ([P4est.jl](https://github.com/trixi-framework/P4est.jl) / [P4est_jll](https://github.com/JuliaBinaryWrappers/P4est_jll.jl) / [p4est](https://github.com/cburstedde/p4est))

As it is now, correctly identifying this manually for a project with probably dozens+ of dependencies is quite tedious. Having this information in the Project.toml metadata would enable automated license scanning.

See also [this thread](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/how-does-licensing-work-for-packages-that-wrap-other-licensed-binaries/104350) split off from a discussion about QML.jl.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/1740372238/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044/events","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1044","id":607462973,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU2MDc0NjI5NzM=","number":1044,"title":"Self-sufficient MinGW-based toolchain / packages","user":{"login":"xhochy","id":70274,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcwMjc0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/70274?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy","html_url":"https://github.com/xhochy","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xhochy/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":11,"created_at":"2020-04-27T11:18:47Z","updated_at":"2023-03-06T08:50:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We currently rely on the `msys2` channel for providing a MinGW-based toolchain. The main usage of this is currently to build R and its packages. The `msys2` channel hasn't been updated for more than 3 years and we are also missing some native dependencies like `m2w64-pcre2` in it for the latest R release. To make conda-forge self-sufficient on Windows, we should get rid of the `msys2` channel dependency and build up our own set of packages for this.

There are two approaches I could think of, each with its different problems:

1. Build up our own MinGW toolchain from scratch and compile all packages from source. We could also make the compiler as a matrix so that we build some packages in the same recipe for MSVC and MinGW. The disadvantage here is that we will replicate all the work from MSYS2. On the other side this better fits into our infrastructure and would also allow us to apply our own modifications to the source build.
2. Repackage binaries from [MSYS2](https://www.msys2.org/) as the `msys2` channel has done in the past. This is the easier approach but we need to think of which packages should be included. All? Only a fixed list?

Approach 2 can probably be scripted mostly using the metadata from MSYS2 and would be the path to go for now to get things up and {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1824","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1824/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1824/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1824/events","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io/issues/1824","id":1364663619,"node_id":"I_kwDOAlKGKs5RVx1D","number":1824,"title":"`-ffast-math` in one package might infect all libraries in an environment with inaccurate FPU","user":{"login":"h-vetinari","id":33685575,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMzNjg1NTc1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/33685575?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari","html_url":"https://github.com/h-vetinari","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/h-vetinari/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2022-09-07T13:25:52Z","updated_at":"2023-03-10T05:48:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This [blog post](https://moyix.blogspot.com/2022/09/someones-been-messing-with-my-subnormals.html) raises a number of concerning points, the prime one being:

> when `-ffast-math` is enabled, the compiler will link in a constructor that sets the FTZ/DAZ flags whenever the library is loaded — even on shared libraries, which means that any application that loads that library will have its floating point behavior changed

This is made worse by `-Ofast` (it's gonna make my code faster, right?) turning on `-ffast-math` unconditionally (as in: `-fno-fast-math` has no effect).

I think we should consider stripping out those flags where they exist. The blog also mentions a few ways to analyse whether the shared objects are affected. Perhaps that's something for conda-build to do after verification?

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

The recipe's [run requirements](https://github.com/conda-forge/buildbot-worker-feedstock/blob/d4f3124fc0cac46e51c247ef597427f162a98166/recipe/meta.yaml#L24-L27) are missing that buildbot-worker depends on `autobahn` and `msgpack` as visible [here](https://github.com/buildbot/buildbot/blob/e04a0f1d40d4550451acc7577d8ac2a930a78b03/worker/setup.py#L154-L158).

It probably makes sense to study the setup.py and check if other (conditional) dependencies need to be added, too.

By the, way, I noticed that this exact problem was already [flagged in previous update PRs](https://github.com/conda-forge/buildbot-worker-feedstock/pull/36#issue-1474964026), but the PR was merged regardless. I don't know if that is a problem of an overeager bot, or some other part of the process needs to be adjusted, but it looks to me like that PR at least should have gotten an extended review.

A build that does not involve NumPy emits info/warning messages about NumPy:
```
No numpy version specified in conda_build_config.yaml.  Falling back to default numpy value of 1.11
WARNING:conda_build.metadata:No numpy version specified in conda_build_config.yaml.  Falling back to default numpy value of 1.11
```
### Expected Behavior

No irrelevant NumPy messages. Well, I _think_ they're irrelevant -- I can't think how this information would be useful in this case.

### Steps to Reproduce

In a directory with this `meta.yaml`:
```
package:
  name: aws
  version: 0

requirements:
  run:
    - awscli
```
do `conda build -c conda-forge .`.

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2021-11-25T16:09:36Z","updated_at":"2022-04-29T08:02:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When using double precision float literals, e.g.  `1.4d0`, in my Fortran code, I get warnings like `"unexpected EOF while parsing (<string>, line 1)" on '1.0d0'`.

I guess some parser does not expect this fortran number format? The pyf file looks ok to my eye, but I don't know what else could be broken by this?

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
> test_d0_float_literals.f90
module test_d0_float_literals
    implicit none
    double precision, parameter :: myparam=1.0d0
end module test_d0_float_literals

> "f2py .\test_d0_float_literals.f90 -m test_float_literals -h test_float_literals.pyf" generates
> test_float_literals.pyf

!    -*- f90 -*-
! Note: the context of this file is case sensitive.

python module test_float_literals ! in 
    interface  ! in :test_float_literals
        module test_d0_float_literals ! in :test_float_literals:.\test_d0_float_literals.f90
            double precision, parameter,optional :: myparam=1.0d0
        end module test_d0_float_literals
    end interface 
end python module test_float_literals

! This file was auto-generated with f2py (version:1.21.4).
! See http://cens.ioc.ee/projects/f2py2e/
```


### Error message:

```shell
> Output from f2py .\test_d0_float_literals.f90 -m test_float_literals -h test_float_literals.pyf

Reading fortran codes...
        Reading file '.\\test_d0_float_literals.f90' (format:free)
Post-processing...
        Block: test_float_literals
                        Block: test_d0_float_literals
In: :test_float_literals:.\test_d0_float_literals.f90:test_d0_float_literals
get_parameters: got "unexpected EOF while parsing (<string>, line 1)" on '1.0d0'
Post-processing (stage 2)...
        Block: test_float_literals
                Block: unknown_interface
                        Block: test_d0_float_literals
Saving signatures to file ".\test_float_literals.pyf"
```


### NumPy/Python version information:

submitting a...** - bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior Using conda run -n for environments, when shell is in specified environment, leads to default conda interpreter being used instead of environment interpreter. This leads to python not finding packages e.g. numpy. ### Steps to Reproduce On w10 with miniconda + at least 1 environment. Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched for other issues and couldn't find a solution or duplication
- [X] I already searched in Google and didn't find any good information or help
- [X] I looked at the [docs](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/user-guide/index.html) and didn't see anything to help

### What happened?

Hi Python extension VSCode dev here 👋 Consider the following scenario:

1. Create conda environment:

```
conda create -n hello python
```
2. Activate the environment using `conda activate hello`.
3. Install a module say `black`:
```
python -m pip install black
```
4. Use the following to check if module is installed. Notice it is detected.
```
python -m black -h
```
5. Prefix the command with `conda run` and check again:
```
conda run -n hello python -m black -h
```
Output:
```
ERROR conda.cli.main_run:execute(33): Subprocess for 'conda run ['python', '-m', 'black', '-h']' command failed.  (See above for error)
~\Miniconda3\python.exe: No module named black
```

6. Now deactivate the environment.
```
conda deactivate
```
7.  Run command mentioned in step `5.` again. Notice black module is now detected.

We would've expected step `4.` and `5.` to have the same behavior, but it seems using `conda run` on top of `conda activate` maybe kind of double activates the environment so installed modules can't be accessed.

### Additional Context

We have switched to using `conda run` in our extension for running files or installing modules, this is being problematic as we also send a command to activate the environment when a terminal is opened. (See https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-python/issues/18639, https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-python/issues/18634, https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-python/issues/18638)

When using double precision float literals, e.g.  `1.4d0`, in my Fortran code, I get warnings like `"unexpected EOF while parsing (<string>, line 1)" on '1.0d0'`.

I guess some parser does not expect this fortran number format? The pyf file looks ok to my eye, but I don't know what else could be broken by this?

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
> test_d0_float_literals.f90
module test_d0_float_literals
    implicit none
    double precision, parameter :: myparam=1.0d0
end module test_d0_float_literals

> "f2py .\test_d0_float_literals.f90 -m test_float_literals -h test_float_literals.pyf" generates
> test_float_literals.pyf

!    -*- f90 -*-
! Note: the context of this file is case sensitive.

python module test_float_literals ! in 
    interface  ! in :test_float_literals
        module test_d0_float_literals ! in :test_float_literals:.\test_d0_float_literals.f90
            double precision, parameter,optional :: myparam=1.0d0
        end module test_d0_float_literals
    end interface 
end python module test_float_literals

! This file was auto-generated with f2py (version:1.21.4).
! See http://cens.ioc.ee/projects/f2py2e/
```


### Error message:

```shell
> Output from f2py .\test_d0_float_literals.f90 -m test_float_literals -h test_float_literals.pyf

Reading fortran codes...
        Reading file '.\\test_d0_float_literals.f90' (format:free)
Post-processing...
        Block: test_float_literals
                        Block: test_d0_float_literals
In: :test_float_literals:.\test_d0_float_literals.f90:test_d0_float_literals
get_parameters: got "unexpected EOF while parsing (<string>, line 1)" on '1.0d0'
Post-processing (stage 2)...
        Block: test_float_literals
                Block: unknown_interface
                        Block: test_d0_float_literals
Saving signatures to file ".\test_float_literals.pyf"
```

Describe the issue:

The following works fine in NumPy < 1.22, but fails in 1.22.0 and 1.22.1. Looks related to #20709.

Fortran code:

```fortran
subroutine foo(is_,ie_,arr)
 implicit none
 integer :: is_,ie_ 
 real*8 :: arr(is_:ie_)
end
```

Compile via f2py:

```bash
$ f2py -c f2py_bug.f90
```

Try to execute with negative bounds:

```bash
$ python -c "import untitled, numpy; arr = numpy.ones(12); untitled.foo(is_=-1, ie_=10, arr=arr)"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
untitled.error: (shape(arr, 0) == 1 + ie_ - is_) failed for 2nd keyword ie_: foo:ie_=10
```

Works with positive bounds (but gives wrong results in my case):

```bash
$ python -c "import untitled, numpy; arr = numpy.ones(12); untitled.foo(is_=0, ie_=11, arr=arr)"
# no exception
```

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
(see above)
```

[x] I read [the conda-forge documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/user/introduction.html#how-can-i-install-packages-from-conda-forge) and could not find the solution for my problem there.

Issue:
Apparently, fpm is not packaged for Windows on conda-forge
```
>conda search fpm
Loading channels: done
No match found for: fpm. Search: *fpm*
# Name                       Version           Build  Channel
r-leafpm                       0.1.0  r351h6115d3f_0  conda-forge

```
r-leafpm is another unrelated package.
This is on Windows 10, conda 4.10.1
This is confirmed by the "platforms" badge in your Readme, which only mention linux and osx.

Is a Windows build going to be available via conda? I noticed that Windows binaries are being published, so there does not seem to be a technical hurdle?
